elements.sent-2411-001: In previous works, we analysed the internal shear layers excited by a viscous forcing (longitudinal libration) in a spherical shell geometry (He et al., J.. Fluid Mech.
elements.sent-2411-002: We now consider the stronger inviscid forcing corresponding to the vertical oscillation of the inner core.
elements.sent-2411-003: We limit our analysis to two-dimensional geometries but examine three different configurations: freely-propagating wave beams in an unbounded domain and two wave patterns (a periodic orbit and an attractor) in a cylindrical shell geometry.
elements.sent-2411-004: The asymptotic structures of the internal shear layers are assumed to follow the similarity solution of Moore \& Saffman (Phil.. Trans.
elements.sent-2411-005: A, 264 (1156), 1969, 597-634) in the small viscous limit.
elements.sent-2411-006: The two undefined parameters of the similarity solution (singularity strength and amplitude) are derived by asymptotically matching the similarity solution with the inviscid solution.
elements.sent-2411-007: For each case, the derivation of the latter is achieved either through separation of variables combined with analytical continuation or the method of characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-008: Global inviscid solutions, when obtained, closely match numerical solutions for small Ekman numbers far from the critical lines, while viscous asymptotic solutions show excellent performance near those lines.
elements.sent-2411-009: The amplitude scalings of the internal shear layers excited by an inviscid forcing are found to be divergent as the Ekman number $E$ decreases, specifically $O(E^{-1/6 )$ for the critical point singularity and $O(E^{-1/3 )$ for attractors, in contrast to the convergent scalings found for a viscous forcing.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Inertial waves are ubiquitous in rotating fluids.
elements.sent-2411-011: They are associated with the Coriolis restoring force.
elements.sent-2411-012: They propagate at a fixed angle $\theta_c$ relative to the plane perpendicular to the rotation axis that only depends on the inertial wave frequency $\omega^*$ through the dispersion relation $\theta_c=\arccos({\omega^*/(2\Omega^*) )$ ($\Omega^*$ being the rotation rate) \citep{greenspan1968theory .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This angle is conserved when waves reflect on boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-014: Owing to this property, the width of an inertial wave beam contracts or expands, depending on the inclination of the boundary relative to the propagation angle $\theta_c$ \citep{phillipsEnergyTransferRotating1963 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The contraction effect is responsible for the creation of inviscid singularities on the critical characteristics, such as the tangent lines from the critical points on a convex boundary \citep{ledizesCriticalSlopeSingularities2024 , and the attractors inside a bounded domain \citep{ogilvie2020internal .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Inviscid singularities can be understood as the outcome of the hyperbolic character of the Poincar\'e equation, namely, the ability to transport singularities \citep{kerswellInternalShearLayers1995 and to form an ill-posed Cauchy problem in closed domains \citep{Rieutord2000 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In numerical simulations and experiments, these inviscid singularities are smoothed out by viscosity, giving rise to oscillatory internal shear layers around the critical characteristics \citep{kerswellInternalShearLayers1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: These layers become the dominant feature of a global viscous solution when inviscid singularities are present.
elements.sent-2411-019: The internal shear layers associated with a critical slope singularity have been experimentally observed in both rotating \citep{greenspan1968theory and stratified \citep{Mowbray1967,zhangExperimentalStudyInternal2007 fluids, owing to the similarity between the dispersion relation of inertial waves and that of internal gravity waves.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Similarly in a bounded domain, attractors give rise to internal shear layers that have been observed in various configurations \citep{maasObservationInternalWave1997,Hazewinkel2008,Klein2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The mathematical description of the internal oscillatory shear layers relies on local asymptotic analysis in the limit of the small Ekman number $E$ (defined as the ratio between the viscous term and the Coriolis term, see below).
elements.sent-2411-022: Although different scalings have been identified for the width of these layers in forced and eigenvalue problems, including $E^{1/3}$, $E^{1/4}$, $E^{1/5}$ and $E^{1/6}$ \citep{kerswellInternalShearLayers1995,rieutordAxisymmetricInertialModes2018 , the scaling in $E^{1/3}$ appears as the natural scaling in a forced problem for the width of the internal shear layer associated with a critical slope singularity or an attractor.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For the former, the scaling was derived by dominance balance \citep{waltonWavesThinRotating1975,waltonViscousShearLayers1975,kerswellInternalShearLayers1995 , and numerically validated \citep{linLibrationdrivenInertialWaves2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: For the latter, it was inferred by \citet{ogilvieWaveAttractorsAsymptotic2005 and numerically validated by \citet{grisouardNumericalSimulationTwodimensional2008 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The viscous structure of these layers has been the subject of many works.
elements.sent-2411-026: The first theoretical work in an oscillatory context goes probably back to \citet{waltonWavesThinRotating1975 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The building block is now believed to be a class of similarity solutions that was introduced by \citet{mooreStructureFreeVertical1969 in rotating fluids and \citet{thomasSimilaritySolutionViscous1972 in stratified fluids.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: These solutions depend on an index $m$ that is related to the strength of the underlying inviscid singularity \citep{ledizesCriticalSlopeSingularities2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The similarity solutions were initially used to describe in the far field the solution generated by a localized oscillating source \citep{hurleyGenerationInternalWaves1997,voisinLimitStatesInternal2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this context, \citet{machicoaneInfluenceMultipoleOrder2015 have shown that the index $m$ is linked to multipolar character of the source.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The use of the similarity solutions to describe the oscillatory internal shear layers generated from a critical point was first made by \citet{ledizesInternalShearLayers2017 in the case of a viscous forcing for a librating object in an unbounded domain.}
elements.sent-2411-032: For the attractor case, they used original ideas that were introduced in an inclined rotating square subject to a body forcing by \citet{ogilvieWaveAttractorsAsymptotic2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: These ideas will also be used in the present study.
elements.sent-2411-034: In our previous works \citep{ledizesInternalShearLayers2017,heInternalShearLayers2022,heInternalShearLayers2023 , the internal shear layers were generated by libration, that is by a viscous forcing at the critical point.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In the present study, we consider an inviscid forcing, specifically vertical oscillation, where internal shear layers are forced through pressure coupling.
elements.sent-2411-036: For such a forcing, the nature of the internal shear layers generated from a critical point is expected to be different.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our analysis will therefore go through the derivation of global inviscid solutions before considering internal shear layers.
elements.sent-2411-038: It will be limited to two-dimensional geometries.
elements.sent-2411-039: We start with the classical problem of an oscillating cylinder in an unbounded domain.
elements.sent-2411-040: The inviscid problem has been solved in a stratified fluid by \citet{hurleyGenerationInternalWaves1997a .}
elements.sent-2411-041: We provide here the inviscid solution in a rotating fluid using the same approach.
elements.sent-2411-042: This solution is singular along the characteristics issued from the critical points.
elements.sent-2411-043: From the behavior of the solution close to the singularity, we obtain the index and the amplitude of the similarity solution that describes the viscous structure of the internal shear layer.
elements.sent-2411-044: The approximation that is obtained is compared with viscous numerical results obtained for very small Ekman numbers.
elements.sent-2411-045: We then consider the cylindrical shell geometry.
elements.sent-2411-046: The ray patterns strongly depend on the frequency.
elements.sent-2411-047: For a frequency $\omega^*= \sqrt{2}\,\Omega^*$, the propagation is at 45 degrees with respect to the rotation axis.
elements.sent-2411-048: We first consider this case for which the ray trajectories are periodic.
elements.sent-2411-049: A global inviscid solution is obtained by writing done the conditions of reflection on each trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-050: This solution is singular on the critical lines.
elements.sent-2411-051: To obtain an approximation valid close to these lines, we will adapt the method used in \citet{heInternalShearLayers2022 for a viscous forcing.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Both the index and the amplitude of the similarity solutions needed to build this asymptotic solution are obtained by matching this solution with the global inviscid solution.
elements.sent-2411-053: Again, the theoretical results are compared with numerical results.
elements.sent-2411-054: In the last section, we consider the case of an attractor.
elements.sent-2411-055: An asymptotic solution is obtained close to the attractor using the analysis developed in \citet{heInternalShearLayers2023 , and then compared to numerical results.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The paper is concluded by summarizing the main results, emphasizing the difference between inviscid and viscous forcings, and discussing the applicability of the results in a three-dimensional context.
elements.sent-2411-057: We consider the internal shear layers generated by a two-dimensional cylindrical body oscillating vertically in either an unbounded or bounded domain.
elements.sent-2411-058: For the former, the configuration is a circular cylinder immersed in an unbounded fluid, as shown in figure \ref{fig:config_cylinder_vertical_oscillation_unbounded_domain ; for the latter, the geometry is a cylindrical annulus, as shown in figure \ref{fig:config_cylinder_vertical_oscillation .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In both cases, the cylinders axes are horizontal and extend to infinity, making the problems two-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-060: We adopt a Cartesian coordinate system, where the axis $Oz$ is vertical to the horizontal plane $Oxy$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The cylinders axes are along the axis $Oy$ such that cross-sections lie in the vertical plane $Oxz$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The flow is that of an incompressible fluid with constant kinematic viscosity $\nu^*$, rotating around the axis $Oz$ with a uniform rotation rate $\Omega^*$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Although the flow field only depends on $x$ and $z$, it has three velocity components.
elements.sent-2411-064: Due to the two dimensional nature of the problem, we can use a polar coordinate system to describe the flow field, with the radial coordinate $\varrho=\sqrt{x^2+z^2}$ and angular coordinate $\vartheta=\arctan{(z/x)}$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The flow domain is defined by $\varrho \in[\varrho_i^*, \varrho_o^*]$, where $\varrho_o^*$ is infinite for the unbounded case.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the bounded case, we write $[\varrho_i^*, \varrho_o^*]=[\eta\varrho^*,\varrho^*]$, with $\eta \in]0,1[$ defining the aspect ratio.
elements.sent-2411-067: For convenience of comparison, the radial range of the unbounded domain is chosen as $[\varrho_i^*, \varrho_o^*]=[\eta\varrho^*,+\infty]$, with the same inner radius as the bounded domain.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{config_cylinder_vertical_oscillation_unbounded_domain.pdf} \caption{A cylinder oscillating vertically in an unbounded rotating fluid.} \label{fig:config_cylinder_vertical_oscillation_unbounded_domain} \end{subfigure} ~~~ \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{config_cylinder_vertical_oscillation.pdf} ~ \caption{A cylindrical shell domain of rotating fluid with an inner cylinder oscillating vertically.} \label{fig:config_cylinder_vertical_oscillation} \end{subfigure} \caption{Two dimensional configurations in unbounded $(a)$ and bounded $(b)$ domains.} \label{fig:config}
elements.sent-2411-068: For both configurations, lengths are non-dimensionalised by $\varrho^*$, resulting in non-dimensional radial ranges of $[\eta,1]$ for the bounded domain and $[\eta,+\infty]$ for the unbounded domain.
elements.sent-2411-069: Time is non-dimensionalised by the angular period $1/\Omega^*$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Using these scales, the Ekman number is defined as \begin{equation}E=\frac{\nu^*}{\Omega^*\varrho^{*2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-071: The imposed harmonic forcing is the vertical oscillation of the cylinder in the unbounded domain or the inner cylinder in the bounded domain (see red arrows in figure \ref{fig:config ), with the displacement amplitude $\varepsilon=\varepsilon^*/\varrho^*$ ($\varepsilon\ll 1$) and the frequency $\omega=\omega^*/\Omega^*$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: As in our previous works \citep{ledizesInternalShearLayers2017,heInternalShearLayers2022,heInternalShearLayers2023 , we focus on the linear harmonic response and consider solutions in the form \begin{equation}(\boldsymbol{v},p)\rme^{-\rmi\omega t}+c.c.,\end{equation} where $c.c.$ denotes the complex conjugate.}
elements.sent-2411-074: As the oscillations are assumed to be very small ($\varepsilon \ll 1$), the (inner) cylinder can be assumed fixed at leading order.
elements.sent-2411-075: The boundary conditions at the surface of the (inner) cylinder read \begin{equation}\label{eq:boundary_condition} \boldsymbol{v}=\boldsymbol{e}_z, \quad at \quad \varrho=\sqrt{x^2+z^2}=\eta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: We apply no-slip boundary conditions at the outer cylinder.
elements.sent-2411-077: In the unbounded case, we apply a condition of radiation which states that the field should be composed of outgoing waves at infinity.
elements.sent-2411-078: In a viscous fluid, this condition is equivalent to the vanishing of the solution at infinity.
elements.sent-2411-079: In the numerical code, the condition of radiation will be applied by damping the waves propagating after a large but finite radial distance.
elements.sent-2411-080: We shall confine ourselves to the inertial wave regime, namely, $0<\omega<2$, within which the dominant structures of the linear response are internal shear layers.
elements.sent-2411-081: The numerical method from our previous work \citep{heInternalShearLayers2023 is adopted here.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The governing equations (\ref{eq:governing_equations ) are numerically solved in polar coordinates $(\varrho, \vartheta)$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The right-hand side terms $f^{\psi _l$ and $f^{\chi _l$ represent a sponger layer absorbing outgoing waves and will be specified later.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The spectral equations (\ref{eq:spectral_equations ) are supplemented with boundary conditions at both ends of the radial direction.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The vertical oscillation (\ref{eq:boundary_condition ) at one end yields the following boundary condition \begin{equation}\psi_l=\frac{\varrho}{2}\delta_{1l}, \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}\psi_l}{\mathrm{d}\varrho}=\frac{1}{2}\delta_{1l}, \quad \chi_l=0 \quad at \quad \varrho=\eta.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The boundary condition at the other end is \begin{equation}\psi_l=\frac{\mathrm{d}\psi_l}{\mathrm{d}\varrho}=\chi_l=0 \quad at \quad \varrho= 1 \quad or \quad \varrho\rightarrow +\infty,\end{equation} for the bounded or unbounded domain respectively.
elements.sent-2411-089: Since the forcing is imposed at $l=1$ for $\psi$, only the Fourier components $\psi_1,\chi_2,\psi_3,\chi_4,\cdots$ are excited.
elements.sent-2411-090: Special treatment is required for the unbounded domain, which is truncated at a finite radius.
elements.sent-2411-091: The position of the outer end is chosen to be far from the source, within the limit of numerical resources.
elements.sent-2411-092: A sponge layer is added near the outer end to absorb all outgoing waves.
elements.sent-2411-093: We directly implement the sponge layer in the spectral equations (\ref{eq:spectral_equations ) through the right-hand terms $f^\psi_l$ and $f^\chi_l$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: They take the form of a damping function as follows \begin{subeqnarray f^\psi_l=\frac{1 {2\Upsilon}\left[1+\tanh{\left(\frac{\varrho-\varrho_s {\Lambda}\right)}\right]\nabla_l^2\psi_l, f^\chi_l=\frac{1 {2\Upsilon}\left[1+\tanh{\left(\frac{\varrho-\varrho_s {\Lambda}\right)}\right]\chi_l.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The parameter $\varrho_s$ is the position where the sponge layer is centred and should be close to the outer end.
elements.sent-2411-096: The parameter $\Lambda$ controls the domain range affected by the sponge layer.
elements.sent-2411-097: Suitable values of $\varrho_s$ and $\Lambda$ are chosen by trial and error.
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, the spectral equations (\ref{eq:spectral_equations ) are truncated at the Fourier order $L$ and further discretized using the Chebyshev collocation method, with the derivatives relative to $\varrho$ replaced by the Chebyshev differentiation matrices of order $N+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-099: The resulting block tridiagonal system is solved by the block tridiagonal algorithm as described in \citet{heInternalShearLayers2023 .}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Inviscid framework in global and local coordinate systems
elements.sent-2411-100: In this part, we are going to summarize the general inviscid framework in global and local coordinate systems that will be freque+ntly adopted when developing theories for different configurations.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{unbounded_domain_tangent_characteristics.pdf} \caption{Tangent characteristic lines (blue dashed lines) of a cylinder in an unbounded domain. $(x_\pm, z_\pm)$ are characteristic coordinates. $(x_\parallel, x_\perp)$ are local coordinates attached to critical rays $L_1-L_4$ in the first quadrant.} \label{fig:unbounded_domain_tangent_characteristics}
elements.sent-2411-101: In the inviscid case ($E= 0$), the governing equations (\ref{eq:governing_equations ) can be recast into the famous Poincar\'e equation in the Cartesian coordinate system \begin{equation}\label{eq:poincare_equation} \frac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial x^2}+\left(1-\frac{4}{\omega^2}\right)\frac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial z^2}=0,\end{equation} with the streamfunction $\psi$ defined as \refstepcounter{equation \begin{equation}v_x=-\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial z}, \quad v_z=\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x}. \eqno{(\theequation{a,b})}\end{equation} The velocity component $v_y$ perpendicular to the plane $Oxz$ is related to $v_x$ by \begin{equation}v_y = \frac{2}{\rmi\omega}v_x.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: In the inertial wave regime ($0<\omega<2$), the Poincar\'e equation (\ref{eq:poincare_equation ) is a two-dimensional hyperbolic equation, the solution of which can be described in terms of its characteristics.}
elements.sent-2411-103: As in \citet{voisinBoundaryIntegralsOscillating2021 , the global characteristic coordinates are defined as \refstepcounter{equation \begin{equation}x_\pm=\sin\theta_cx\mp\cos\theta_cz, \quad z_\pm=\pm\cos\theta_c x+\sin\theta_c z, \eqno{(\theequation{a,b})}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\omega=2\cos\theta_c,\end{equation} where $\theta_c$ is the acute angle between the characteristics and the horizontal plane.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: The characteristic coordinates $x_{\pm}$ and $z_{\pm}$ are perpendicular and parallel to the characteristics, respectively; particularly, $|x_\pm|=\eta$ denote the positions of the characteristics tangent to the cylinder (see blue symbols figure \ref{fig:unbounded_domain_tangent_characteristics ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: The canonical form of the Poincar\'e equation (\ref{eq:poincare_equation ) is simply $\p^2\psi/\p x_+ \p x_-=0$; the corresponding solution is separable in terms of $x_\pm$, namely, \begin{equation}\label{eq:global_inviscid_streamfunction} \psi(x_+,x_-)=\psi_+(x_+) + \psi_-(x_-).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The corresponding velocity vector in the $Oxz$ plane is \begin{equation}\label{eq:global_inviscid_velocity} v_{z_+}\boldsymbol{\mathrm{e}}_{z_+}+v_{z_-}\boldsymbol{\mathrm{e}}_{z_-}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:global_inviscid_velocity_streamfunction} v_{z_\pm}(x_\pm)=\frac{d\psi_\pm}{dx_\pm},\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{\mathrm{e _{z_\pm}$ are unit vectors in the $z_\pm$ directions.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: The velocity vector can be projected into the Cartesian coordinates, to yield the velocity components in the latter \refstepcounter{equation \begin{equation}v_x=(v_{z_+}-v_{z_-})\cos\theta_c, v_z=(v_{z_+}+v_{z_-})\sin\theta_c, v_y = \frac{2}{\rmi\omega}v_x=-\rmi(v_{z_+}-v_{z_-}). \eqno{(\theequation{a,b,c})}\end{equation}.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{local_coordinates_orientation.pdf} \caption{Critical rays with four different propagation directions (in red) and their local coordinates (in black). Schematic of the reflection on a boundary is shown in orange.} \label{fig:local_coordinates}
elements.sent-2411-108: Usually, the inviscid solution may become singular on critical characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-109: In this case, it is preferable to describe the solution in terms of local coordinates ($x_\parallel, x_\perp$) around the critical characteristics, where $x_\parallel$ measures the distance to the source along the critical characteristic and $x_\perp$ the displacement relative to it.
elements.sent-2411-110: The orientations of the local coordinates ($x_\parallel, x_\perp$) depend on the direction of the wave propagation on the critical characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-111: For a fixed frequency $\omega$ that corresponds to a fixed $\theta_c$, inertial waves propagate in four possible directions, which are $\theta_c$, $\pi-\theta_c$, $\pi+\theta_c$ and $2\pi-\theta_c$ with respect to the positive horizontal direction (see red symbols in figure \ref{fig:local_coordinates ).}
elements.sent-2411-112: The direction of the local parallel coordinate $x_\parallel$ follows the propagation direction; the orientation of the perpendicular coordinate $x_\perp$ is chosen such that it does not change during propagation (see black symbols in figure \ref{fig:local_coordinates ).}
elements.sent-2411-113: In these local frames, the streamfunction is related to the local velocity components $(v_\parallel, v_\perp)$ through \refstepcounter{equation \begin{equation}v_\parallel=\epsilon\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x_\perp}, \quad v_\perp=-\epsilon\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x_\parallel}, \eqno{(\theequation{a,b})}\end{equation} where $\epsilon=1$ for the rays with the inclined angles $\pi-\theta_c$ and $2\pi-\theta_c$, and $\epsilon=-1$ for the rays with inclined angles $\theta_c$ and $\pi-\theta_c$ (see figure \ref{fig:local_coordinates ).}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In the inviscid case, the streamfunction and the parallel velocities depend on the local perpendicular coordinate $x_\perp$ only, namely, \refstepcounter{equation \begin{equation}\psi(x_\perp), \quad v_\parallel(x_\perp)=\epsilon\frac{d\psi(x_\perp)}{d x_\perp}. \eqno{(\theequation{a,b})}\end{equation} The velocity components $(v_x, v_z)$ in the Cartesian coordinates are obtained through projecting $v_\parallel\boldsymbol{\mathrm{e _\parallel$ into the latter.}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Notably, $v_y$ is related to $v_\parallel$ through a phase shift, namely, \begin{equation}v_{y}=\pm\mathrm{i}v_\parallel. \label{eq:vy}\end{equation} The sign of the phase is $``+"$ for the rays with the inclined angles $\pi-\theta_c$ and $\pi+ \theta_c$, and it is $``-"$ for the rays with the inclined angles $\theta_c$ and $2\pi-\theta_c$ (see figure \ref{fig:local_coordinates ).}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Viscous similarity solution describing internal shear layers
elements.sent-2411-116: Now we briefly describe the general asymptotic structure of internal shear layers, irrespective of the configurations.
elements.sent-2411-117: We only show the expressions in two dimensions that are relevant to the current work.
elements.sent-2411-118: Similar descriptions can be found in our previous works \citep{ledizesInternalShearLayers2017,heInternalShearLayers2022,heInternalShearLayers2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: In the small viscosity limit ($E\rightarrow 0$), the quantitative feature of the internal shear layers can be described by the famous similarity solution of \citet{mooreStructureFreeVertical1969 , characterised by a width scaling as $E^{1/3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: This similarity solution is expressed in the local coordinates $(x_\parallel, x_\perp)$ described above.
elements.sent-2411-121: A similarity variable is introduced as \begin{equation}\zeta=\frac{x_{\perp}}{E^{1/3}}\left(\frac{2\sin\theta_c}{x_\parallel}\right)^{1/3}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-122: At leading order, the main velocity component is along the critical characteristics, namely $v_\parallel$, which takes the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:similarity_solution} v_{\parallel}=C_0H_m(x_\parallel,x_\perp)=C_0\left(\frac{x_\parallel}{2\sin\theta_c}\right)^{-m/3}h_m(\zeta),\end{equation} with the special function introduced by \citet{mooreStructureFreeVertical1969 \begin{equation}h_m(\zeta)=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}m\pi/2}}{(m-1)!}\int^{+\infty}_{0}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}p\zeta-p^3}p^{m-1}dp.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Owing to the fluid rotation, the similarity solution also possesses a velocity component perpendicular to the plane ($\boldsymbol{e _{\parallel}$, $\boldsymbol{e _\perp$).}}
elements.sent-2411-125: It is related to $v_\parallel$ by a simple phase shift according the relation (\ref{eq:vy ).}
elements.sent-2411-126: There are two free parameters in the similarity solution (\ref{eq:similarity_solution ): the amplitude $C_0$ and the index $m$.}
elements.sent-2411-127: The calculation of these parameters for each configuration constitutes the core of our work.
elements.sent-2411-128: It will mainly follow the line of our previous works \citep{ledizesInternalShearLayers2017,heInternalShearLayers2022,heInternalShearLayers2023 performed for a viscous forcing.}
elements.sent-2411-129: One can first note that the similarity solution is indeed associated with an inviscid singularity, which is visible when considering the outer limit ($|\eta| \rightarrow \infty$) of the similarity solution \begin{equation}\label{eq:outer_limit_of_similarity_solution} v_{\parallel}\sim \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} C_0x_\perp^{-m}E^{m/3}, \zeta\rightarrow +\infty; [2pt] C_0(-x_\perp)^{-m}\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}m\pi}E^{m/3}, \zeta\rightarrow -\infty. \end{array}\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Note the behavior in $|x_\perp|^{-m}$ on both sides of the singularity.
elements.sent-2411-131: The index $m$ is then directly related to the strength of the underlying inviscid singularity.
elements.sent-2411-132: Note also the phase shift in $e^{-{\rm i m\pi}$ for $x_\perp <0$.}
elements.sent-2411-133: This phase shift is associated with the direction of propagation of the singular field, which has to be in the direction of $+ {\bf e _\parallel$ \citep{ledizesCriticalSlopeSingularities2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-134: The similarity solution (\ref{eq:similarity_solution ) can be expressed in terms of a streamfunction $\psi$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Integration of the similarity solution (\ref{eq:similarity_solution ) leads to \begin{equation}\psi=\epsilon\frac{C_0E^{1/3}}{m-1}H_{m-1}(x_\parallel,x_\perp).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Note that, the streamfunction $\psi$ is $O(E^{1/3 )$ smaller than $v_\parallel$.}
elements.sent-2411-137: In a bounded domain, the self-similar beam is expected to reflect on the boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-138: The reflection law, based on the preservation of the self-similar structure and the non-penetrability condition, has been discussed in \citet{ledizesReflectionOscillatingInternal2020,heInternalShearLayers2022,heInternalShearLayers2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-139: For an incident beam $v_{\parallel ^{(i) =C_0^{(i) H_m(x_{\parallel ^{(i) ,x_{\perp ^{(i) )$ and its reflected counterpart $v_{\parallel ^{(r) =C_0^{(r) H_m(x_{\parallel ^{(r) ,x_{\perp ^{(r) )$, the conservation of the similarity variable and the non-penetrability condition lead to relations for the travelled distance and amplitude before and after the reflection \refstepcounter{equation \begin{equation}\frac{x_{\parallel b}^{(r)}}{x_{\parallel b}^{(i)}}=\alpha^3, \quad \frac{C_0^{(r)}}{C_0^{(i)}}=\alpha^{m-1}, \eqno{(\theequation{a,b})}\end{equation} where the subscript $b$ indicates that the values are taken at the reflection point.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: The reflection factor $\alpha$ is given by \begin{equation}\alpha=\frac{\sin\theta^{(r)}}{\sin\theta^{(i)}},\end{equation} where $\theta^{(r)}$ and $\theta^{(i)}$ represent the angles of the reflected and incident beams relative to the tangent surface at the reflection point (see orange symbols in figure \ref{fig:local_coordinates ).}

elements.sent-2411-329: Using asymptotic analysis and numerical integration of the linearized governing equations, we have studied the internal shear layers generated in a rotating fluid by vertical oscillations of a two-dimensional cylinder, both in an unbounded and in a bounded domain where the outer boundary is a fixed cylinder of same axis.
elements.sent-2411-330: We have shown that these internal shear layers can always be described by the class of similarity solutions introduced by \citet{mooreStructureFreeVertical1969 .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Our main work has been to find the index (or singularity strength) $m$ and amplitude $C_0$ characterizing these solutions for the different configurations that we have considered.
elements.sent-2411-332: This has been done by various techniques.
elements.sent-2411-333: In the unbounded case and in the bounded case with periodic orbits, we have first obtained a global inviscid solution, then performed the asymptotic matching of this solution with the viscous approximation deduced by propagating the similarity solution from the critical point.
elements.sent-2411-334: In both cases, we have compared the inviscid solution and the viscous approximation with numerical results obtained for small Ekman numbers, and an excellent agreement has been observed.
elements.sent-2411-335: In the bounded case with attractor, only a local inviscid approximation close to attractor has been derived from which the adequate viscous approximation describing the internal shear layer has been obtained.
elements.sent-2411-336: This approximation has been shown to capture the behaviour of the numerical solution close to the attractor.
elements.sent-2411-337: In all cases, the width of the internal shear layer is $O(E^{1/3 )$, as expected from the scaling of the similarity solution.}
elements.sent-2411-338: However, different Ekman scalings for the amplitude $C_0$ have been obtained, with a scaling in $E^{-1/6}$ for the internal shear layer created from a critical point, and in $E^{-1/3}$ for the internal shear layer close to the attractor.
elements.sent-2411-339: These large amplitudes are specific to the inviscid forcing.
elements.sent-2411-340: For a viscous forcing, weaker amplitudes in $E^{1/12}$ and $E^{1/6}$ were obtained for the internal shear layers near a critical line and an attractor, respectively \citep{ledizesInternalShearLayers2017,heInternalShearLayers2022,heInternalShearLayers2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-341: These differences can be directly attributed to the different index $m$ obtained for the similarity solution describing the internal shear layer, and to the strength of the forcing, as $C_0$ is directly obtained by the relation \begin{equation}C_0\sim O(A_F E^{-m/3}),\end{equation} where $A_F$ denotes the forcing magnitude which is $O(E^{1/2 )$ for a viscous forcing and $O(1)$ for an inviscid forcing.}
elements.sent-2411-342: For the inviscid forcing, we have shown that $m=1/2$ for the critical point singularity and $m=1$ for the attractor, while for the viscous forcing, it was $m=5/4$ for the critical point singularity and $m=1$ for the attractor.
elements.sent-2411-343: The change of scaling of $C_0$ around an attractor between inviscid and viscous forcings is therefore only due to the change of the strength of the forcing, as the internal shear layer keeps the same similarity structure with $m=1$.
elements.sent-2411-344: This is different for the internal shear layer issued from a critical point.
elements.sent-2411-345: In the viscous case, \citet{ledizesInternalShearLayers2017 obtained an index $m$ larger than 1, that is a singularity stronger than that of the attractor, while $m$ is smaller than 1 in the inviscid case.}

elements.section-2411-009: Numerical solutions for different types of attractors
elements.sent-2411-523: In this section, we consider the numerical solutions obtained for frequencies and aspect ratios for which the attractor has particular properties.
elements.sent-2411-524: In \S~\ref{app:attractor_with_phase_shift , we consider an attractor with a phase shift.}
elements.sent-2411-525: In \S~\ref{app:attractor_attraction1 and \S~\ref{app:attractor_attraction0 , we consider an attractor without phase shift but for which the contraction factor is either 1 or 0.}}
elements.sent-2411-526: This type of attractors are obtained on the border of the frequency range of existence of a given attractor.
elements.sent-2411-527: Here we consider the frequencies $\omega_l=0.806225774$ and $\omega_r=0.824949354$ which are the limit values for the existence of the equatorial attractor for the aspect ratio $\eta=0.35$ \citep{rieutordInertialWavesRotating2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-528: We can obtain attractors with a phase shift at $\eta=0.44$ and $\omega=1.1329$.
elements.sent-2411-529: As shown in figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_with_phase_shift}$(a)$, both the polar and equatorial attractors have one touching point on the horizontal axis $Ox$.
elements.sent-2411-530: Therefore, the phase shift for them is $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-531: Figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_with_phase_shift}$(b)$ shows the profile $v_y$ on the white cut in figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_with_phase_shift}$(a)$ at $E=10^{-11}$, which illustrates that the solution at the attractor position is much weaker than those at other critical positions.
elements.sent-2411-532: Figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_with_phase_shift}$(c)$ demonstrates that the Ekman number scalings of the velocity amplitude are still $E^{-1/6}$ and $E^{-1/3}$ close to the critical point and attractor respectively.
elements.figure-2411-030: \begin{subfigure}{0.4\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.44_omega=1.1329_E=1e-11_contour.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.6\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.44_omega=1.1329_profiles.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.44_omega=1.1329_scaling.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{Attractors at $\eta=0.44$ and $\omega=1.1329$: $(a)$ contour of numerical $|v_y|$ at $E=10^{-11}$; $(b)$ profile of $v_y$ at $E=10^{-11}$ on the white cut in $(a)$; $(c)$ Ekman number scalings of the velocity amplitude at the critical positions on the white cut in $(a)$.} \label{app-fig:attractor_with_phase_shift}
elements.subsection-2411-011: Extremely weak attractor without phase shift
elements.figure-2411-031: \begin{subfigure}{0.4\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8062_E=1e-11_contour.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.6\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8062_profiles.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.35\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8062_width_scaling.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.35\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8062_amplitude_scaling.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{Equatorial attractor at $\eta=0.35$ and $\omega=0.806225774$: $(a)$ contour of numerical $|v_y|$ at $E=10^{-11}$; $(b)$ profiles of $v_y$ on the white cut in $(a)$ at different Ekman numbers ($x_\infty$, attractor position); $(c-d)$ Ekman number scalings for the width and velocity amplitude of the shear layer around the equatorial attractor. } \label{app-fig:attractor_0.8062}
elements.sent-2411-533: Figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8062 shows the results for the equatorial attractor at $\omega=\omega_l$, for which the contraction factor is close to 1.}
elements.sent-2411-534: The equatorial attractor is then extremely weak with a Lyapunov number close to $0$.
elements.sent-2411-535: For this frequency, the equatorial attractor is composed of two segments connecting the inner core equator with the outer core equator.
elements.sent-2411-536: Figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8062}$(a)$ shows that the response around the equatorial attractor is stronger than around the polar attractor.
elements.sent-2411-537: Its amplitude strongly increases with the Ekman number, as observed in figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8062}$(b)$.
elements.sent-2411-538: As shown in figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8062}$(c,d)$, the width and the velocity amplitude of the internal shear layer around the equatorial attractor scale as $E^{1/4}$ and $E^{-1/2}$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-539: This is clearly different from the scalings in $E^{1/3}$ and $E^{-1/3}$ that we have obtained for an attractor with a contraction factor different from 1.
elements.sent-2411-540: Such scalings were already obtained by \cite{rieutordViscousDissipationTidally2010 for attractors with vanishing Lyapunov number forced by tides.}
elements.sent-2411-541: They explained them by a resonance with an attractor eigenmode.
elements.sent-2411-542: The scaling in amplitude comes from the frequency of this eigenmode which expands as $\omega \sim \omega_l + E^{1/2 \omega_1$, while the scaling in $E^{1/4}$ of the width is directly related to the structure of this eigenmode.}
elements.subsection-2411-012: Extremely strong attractor without phase shift
elements.figure-2411-032: \begin{subfigure}{0.4\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8249_E=1e-11_contour.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.6\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8249_profiles.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.35\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8249_width_scaling.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.35\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{attractor_eta=0.350_omega=0.8249_amplitude_scaling.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{Same caption as in figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8062} but for $\eta=0.35$ and $\omega=0.824949354$.} \label{app-fig:attractor_0.8249}
elements.sent-2411-543: In figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8249 , we show the results for the equatorial attractor at $\omega=\omega_r$ .}
elements.sent-2411-544: For this frequency, the attractor is extremely strong as its Lyapunov number and contraction factor are close to minus infinity and zero respectively.
elements.sent-2411-545: Figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8249}$(a)$ shows that the equatorial attractor touches the inner boundary at the critical point.
elements.sent-2411-546: The singularities associated with the critical point and the attractor have then merged in that case.
elements.sent-2411-547: The velocity profiles for the internal shear layer around the equatorial attractor are shown in figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8249}$(b)$.
elements.sent-2411-548: Figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8249}$(c)$ shows that the width of the internal shear layer still scales with $E^{1/3}$.
elements.sent-2411-549: However, figure \ref{app-fig:attractor_0.8249}$(d)$ shows that the amplitude scaling of the velocity is close to $E^{-0.286}$.
elements.sent-2411-550: This amplitude is smaller than the $E^{-1/3}$ we have found for a regular attractor.
elements.sent-2411-551: Surprisingly, the merging of the singularities has therefore not boosted the response.
elements.sent-2411-552: The amplitude scaling is just in between the $E^{-1/3}$ and $E^{-1/6}$ obtained for the attractor solution and the critical-point solution when they are well separated from each other.. \bibliographystyle{jfm.}
